title: defaultRevList timeout is ineffective — readText() blocks before waitFor() can fire
description: The REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS guard is checked only after `process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText()` returns. On a slow/hung network-mounted repository or a corrupted git index, `readText()` will block indefinitely reading stdout before `waitFor()` is ever evaluated. The comment claims this guards against slow network drives, but the timeout can never interrupt the blocking `readText()` call — the guard only applies to the time spent waiting for the process to exit AFTER readText() has already drained stdout.
suggestedFix: Read stdout on a separate thread (or with a deadline) so the timeout can fire concurrently. The standard pattern is to use `ProcessBuilder` with `inheritIO()` + a separate thread for stdout, or to use `withTimeoutOrNull` around the entire block inside a `Dispatchers.IO` coroutine. The simplest correct fix: launch a background thread to drain stdout into a StringBuilder, call `process.waitFor(timeout, unit)` on the current thread, then destroy-and-return-empty if the timeout fires, or collect the thread result if it completes in time.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at PlanDetectionService.kt lines 401-411. `process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText()` is called synchronously BEFORE `process.waitFor(REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, TimeUnit.SECONDS)`. Since `readText()` blocks the calling thread until the subprocess closes its stdout (i.e., until the process exits), the `waitFor()` call on the next line can never interrupt a hung `git rev-list` process — it will only observe the exit code after `readText()` has already returned. The timeout guard is therefore dead code for the hang scenario it was designed to protect against. On a network-mounted repository or corrupted git index where git hangs indefinitely, `defaultRevList` will block forever. The KDoc comment at line 398 explicitly claims this guards against slow network drives, making the misimplementation a correctness bug. The fix (drain stdout on a background thread, call `waitFor` on the current thread) does not remove any useful behaviour — it makes the stated contract actually true.
recommendedAction: Drain stdout on a background thread: launch a coroutine or Java Thread to read stdout into a StringBuilder, then call `process.waitFor(REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` on the calling thread. If the timeout fires, call `destroyForcibly()` and interrupt/join the reader thread, then return emptyList(). The simplest Kotlin idiom is `val readerThread = thread(start = true) { output.append(process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText()) }; if (!process.waitFor(REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) { process.destroyForcibly(); readerThread.interrupt(); return emptyList() }; readerThread.join(); return output.toString().lines()...`.

title: Swing component (JBCheckBox) constructed on IO background thread in ManualStageDialog
description: Inside the `scope.launch { }` block in the `init` (where `scope` is the caller's `panelScope`, which runs on `Dispatchers.IO`), `JBCheckBox(name, preChecked)` is constructed at line 163 on a background thread. The Swing/AWT threading model requires all Swing component construction and mutation to happen on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT). Constructing a `JBCheckBox` off-EDT causes an unsynchronized access to Swing's component tree, which can corrupt the component state or produce rendering artefacts.
suggestedFix: Move all Swing component construction into the `invokeLater` block that already exists at the end of the `scope.launch`. Compute `stageNames` (a plain `List<String>`) on the IO coroutine, then in `invokeLater` create the `JBCheckBox` instances, assign `stageCheckboxes`, and call `refillSlots()` + `updateOkButton()`. This keeps IO work on IO and Swing construction on the EDT.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at ManualStageDialog.kt lines 143-183. The `scope.launch { }` block runs on whatever dispatcher `scope` (the caller's `panelScope`) is bound to — `panelScope` is declared as `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` in BuildDashboardPanel (line 89). Inside this IO coroutine, `JBCheckBox(name, preChecked)` is constructed at line 163 and `cb.addActionListener { ... }` is called at line 164 — both are Swing component operations that the AWT threading model requires to happen on the EDT. The `invokeLater` at line 179 calls `refillSlots()` and `updateOkButton()` on the EDT, but the checkbox objects themselves were already constructed off-EDT. The existing `invokeLater` is the correct site to move construction into; only `stageNames` (a plain `List<String>`) needs to be computed on IO.
recommendedAction: Compute `stageNames` (pure data) on the IO coroutine. Move `JBCheckBox` construction and `addActionListener` calls into the `invokeLater` block at line 179, before `refillSlots()` and `updateOkButton()`. Assign `stageCheckboxes` inside `invokeLater` so both the construction and the field write happen on the EDT.

title: ManualStageDialog shared fields written from IO coroutine and read from EDT without @Volatile
description: Fields `variables: List<PlanVariableData>`, `stageCheckboxes`, `stageLoadError`, `isLoading`, and `isLoadingStages` are written from within `scope.launch` (IO thread) and then read from the EDT in `populateVariablesEditor`, `populateStageSlot`, `updateOkButton`, and `doOKAction`. None of these fields carry `@Volatile`. Without `@Volatile` or explicit synchronization, the JMM does not guarantee that EDT reads will see the writes made by the IO coroutine, even though the `invokeLater` at the end of the coroutine provides a happens-before for the read paths it dispatches — `doOKAction` reads `variables` and `stageCheckboxes` independently without going through `invokeLater`.
suggestedFix: Add `@Volatile` to all fields written from the IO coroutine and read from the EDT: `variables`, `stageCheckboxes`, `stageLoadError`, `isLoading`, `isLoadingStages`. Alternatively, move all writes into the existing `invokeLater` block so they are confined to the EDT throughout, eliminating the cross-thread visibility gap entirely.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at ManualStageDialog.kt. Fields `variables` (line 104), `isLoading` (line 105), `stageCheckboxes` (line 108), `stageLoadError` (line 110), `isLoadingStages` (line 112) are plain `private var` with no `@Volatile`. They are written from the IO coroutine (`scope.launch`) at lines 147, 153, 157, 168-174, 178. `doOKAction` at line 624 reads `variables.filter { it.isPassword }` directly on the EDT without going through any `invokeLater` barrier, creating a JMM visibility gap. The `invokeLater` at line 179 establishes happens-before only for `refillSlots()` and `updateOkButton()` — not for `doOKAction`. In practice, by the time the user can click OK the `invokeLater` has already delivered, so the temporal window is narrow; this makes it a medium-severity issue rather than high, but it is a genuine JMM violation. Severity is downgraded from High to Medium because the practical race window is very narrow: `doOKAction` is only reachable after the `invokeLater` has delivered (dialog buttons are on EDT, the user must have seen the loaded content), so a torn read is theoretically possible but extremely rare.
recommendedAction: Add `@Volatile` to `variables`, `stageCheckboxes`, `stageLoadError`, `isLoading`, and `isLoadingStages`, OR (cleaner) confine all writes of these fields to the existing `invokeLater` block so they are EDT-only, matching the reads in `populateVariablesEditor`, `populateStageSlot`, `updateOkButton`, and `doOKAction`.

title: BambooServiceImpl.client getter has TOCTOU race — @Volatile does not protect compound check-create-write
description: The `client` getter performs a read-check-write compound operation on `cachedBaseUrl` and `cachedClient` that is not atomic. Two coroutines calling `client` concurrently from `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl || cachedClient == null` as true, both construct a new `BambooApiClient` (creating an additional OkHttpClient derived from `sharedPool`), and then both write to `cachedClient`. The second write discards the first client. More critically, `cachedBaseUrl` is written BEFORE `cachedClient` in the same if-block, so in the window between those two writes a third reader sees a stale `cachedBaseUrl` pointing to the new URL but a `cachedClient` still built for the old URL. In `triggerBuild`, `cachedBaseUrl` is then read directly to form the `link` URL — if the URL was just changed this can produce a mismatched link.
suggestedFix: Guard the entire compound operation with `@Synchronized` on the getter, or replace both `@Volatile` fields with a single `@Volatile` holder of a `Pair<String, BambooApiClient>` so the read-check-replace is a single volatile write. The cleanest fix per the project conventions is a coroutine `Mutex` wrapping the check-and-recreate path since service methods are already `suspend`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 56-72. The `client` getter performs a three-step compound operation: read `cachedBaseUrl`, compare to `url`, then conditionally write `cachedBaseUrl = url` followed by `cachedClient = BambooApiClient(...)`. Two coroutines on `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl` as true simultaneously, both construct a new client, and both write. The `@Volatile` annotations on `cachedBaseUrl` (line 44) and `cachedClient` (line 43) individually guarantee visibility of each field's latest write but do not make the compound read-check-write atomic: one writer's `cachedBaseUrl = url` write lands before the other writer's `cachedClient` assignment, creating a window where a third reader sees the new URL but the old client. The bug is also noted in `triggerBuild` which reads `cachedBaseUrl` to form the link URL. However, in typical usage there is only one configured Bamboo URL that almost never changes, and the client construction is idempotent — the worst outcome is a redundant OkHttpClient allocation. Severity is correctly assessed as Medium.
recommendedAction: Replace the two `@Volatile` fields with a single `@Volatile` holder: `@Volatile private var cachedEntry: Pair<String, BambooApiClient>? = null`. The getter reads the pair atomically, checks `entry?.first == url`, and if mismatched creates a new pair and writes it as a single volatile store. Alternatively, add a `Mutex` and wrap the check-create-write in `mutex.withLock { }` since the callers are already in suspend context.

title: BuildMonitorService shared mutable state has no synchronization — concurrent pollOnce calls corrupt state
description: The fields `previousBuildNumber`, `previousStatus`, `lastLogFetchedForBuild`, `jobOrderCache`, and `poller` are plain (non-`@Volatile`, non-synchronized) `private var` fields. `pollOnce` is a public `suspend fun` called from (a) the `SmartPoller` action running in `cs` (project scope), and (b) `panelScope.launch` (Dispatchers.IO) when the user clicks Refresh, Stop, or Cancel in `BuildDashboardPanel`. These two scopes can execute `pollOnce` concurrently. A race between them on `previousBuildNumber`/`previousStatus` can cause a `BuildFinished` event to be emitted twice for the same build, or suppressed, and `lastLogFetchedForBuild` can be written by one coroutine before the other has fetched all logs — causing the 'retry on partial fetch' logic to never re-run.
evidence:
private var previousBuildNumber: Int? = null
private var previousStatus: BuildStatus? = null
private var lastLogFetchedForBuild: Int? = null
// ...
val isFirstPoll = previousBuildNumber == null
val statusChanged = dto.buildNumber != previousBuildNumber || ...
previousBuildNumber = dto.buildNumber
suggestedFix: Add a `Mutex` (coroutine mutex from `kotlinx.coroutines.sync`) to `BuildMonitorService` and wrap the entire body of `pollOnce` in `mutex.withLock { }`. This ensures only one `pollOnce` executes at a time regardless of which scope calls it, which is the correct semantic since the fields form a consistent unit of state (previousNumber + previousStatus + lastLogFetched must all advance together). Alternatively, mark all fields `@Volatile` and use `compareAndSet` patterns — but the mutex approach is simpler and the lock contention is negligible (30s intervals).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BuildMonitorService.kt. Fields `previousBuildNumber` (line 148), `previousStatus` (line 149), `lastLogFetchedForBuild` (line 150), `jobOrderCache` (line 158) are plain `private var` with no synchronization. `pollOnce` is `public suspend fun` called from two independent coroutine scopes: (1) the `SmartPoller` action lambda at line 188 inside `startPolling`, which runs on `cs` (the service's injected `CoroutineScope`); (2) `panelScope.launch { monitorService.pollOnce(...) }` in BuildDashboardPanel's Refresh action (line 933-935) and Stop action (line 968). These two scopes are independent so concurrent execution is possible. A race between them on `previousBuildNumber`/`previousStatus` can cause `BuildFinished` to be emitted twice for the same build (if both coroutines observe `statusChanged = true` before either writes `previousBuildNumber`) or for `lastLogFetchedForBuild` to be set by one before the other has fetched all logs — skipping the retry path. However, the Refresh button is user-initiated and typically fires outside the 30s polling window, so the overlap is rare in practice. Severity is downgraded from High to Medium.
recommendedAction: Add a `private val pollMutex = Mutex()` to `BuildMonitorService` and wrap the entire body of `pollOnce` in `pollMutex.withLock { ... }`. This ensures the `previousBuildNumber + previousStatus + lastLogFetchedForBuild` fields advance as a consistent unit regardless of which scope calls `pollOnce`.

title: getProjects() silently truncates at 100 projects — no pagination
description: Unlike `getPlans()`, `getBranches()`, `getPlanBranches()`, and `getLinkedRepositories()` — which all use the shared `paginate()` loop — `getProjects()` makes a single GET with `max-results=100` and returns only the first page. Bamboo installations with more than 100 projects will silently return an incomplete list. The `BambooServiceImpl.getProjects()` surface is exposed to the agent (`BambooService.getProjects`) and used for project-key lookups; a user with >100 projects can never find plans belonging to projects beyond the first 100.
suggestedFix: Wrap `getProjects()` in the same `paginate()` helper used by `getPlans()`, using `BambooProjectListResponse` and extracting `it.projects.project` as the page items. The paginator already handles both the offset advancement and the short-page termination condition, so the change is a straightforward mechanical replacement matching the pattern of `getPlans()`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BambooApiClient.kt lines 48-51. `getProjects()` makes a single GET with `max-results=100` and returns only the first page. All sibling listing methods — `getPlans()` (line 41), `getBranches()` (line 83), `getPlanBranches()` (line 522), `getLinkedRepositories()` (line 499) — use the shared `paginate()` helper. The inconsistency is clear and intentional only by omission (no comment explains the single-page design). `getProjects()` is exposed to the agent via `BambooService.getProjects()` and is used for project-key lookups; silently returning at most 100 projects is a functional gap for large Bamboo instances. The `paginate()` helper at line 624 is a direct fit — it takes a `pageSize` and `fetchPage` lambda and handles offset advancement and the short-page termination condition. Since Bamboo project endpoints follow the same pagination contract as plans and branches, this is a safe mechanical change.
recommendedAction: Wrap `getProjects()` in `paginate(pageSize = 100, maxPages = 50, label = "projects") { start -> get<BambooProjectListResponse>("/rest/api/latest/project?max-results=100&start-index=$start").map { it.projects.project } }`, matching the pattern of `getPlans()`.

title: GitRepository.currentRevision and currentBranchName accessed without ReadAction on IO background thread
description: The methods `readActionLocalHead()` (line 810) and `getCurrentBranch()` (line 807) read `GitRepository.currentRevision` and `GitRepository.currentBranchName` respectively. Despite the name `readActionLocalHead`, neither method wraps the access in `ReadAction.compute {}` or the modern `readAction {}` suspend call. Both are called from within `panelScope.launch` (which runs on `Dispatchers.IO`) — specifically in `resolveBranchPlanAndMonitor` (lines 235, 238) and in the Refresh action's `panelScope.launch` (line 934). The IntelliJ Platform requires VCS model reads to be performed inside a read action when accessed from a background thread, or a write-action interleaving can cause a torn read of the repository state.
suggestedFix: Wrap all VCS model reads in `readAction { }` (the non-deprecated suspend variant from `com.intellij.openapi.application.readAction`). Since the callers are already in a suspend context, convert to: `val latestCommit = readAction { getGitRepo()?.currentRevision }.orEmpty()` and similarly for `getCurrentBranch()`. The `readActionLocalHead` naming implies this was intended; the actual `ReadAction` call is simply missing.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BuildDashboardPanel.kt lines 807 and 810. `getCurrentBranch()` returns `getGitRepo()?.currentBranchName` and `readActionLocalHead()` returns `getGitRepo()?.currentRevision` — both without wrapping in `readAction { }`. Both are called from `panelScope.launch` (Dispatchers.IO) in `resolveBranchPlanAndMonitor` at lines 235 and 238, and `getCurrentBranch()` is also called from the Refresh action's `panelScope.launch` at line 934. The bamboo CLAUDE.md explicitly states 'PrBar git reads use `readAction { }`', indicating this is the expected pattern in the codebase. The core CLAUDE.md also notes the deprecation of `runReadAction` in favour of `readAction { }` for 2026.1. However, `GitRepository.currentBranchName` and `currentRevision` in git4idea are properties of `GitRepositoryImpl` backed by `@Volatile` fields updated under the git repository state lock — the IntelliJ Platform formally requires a read action around PSI and file model access, but in practice these git4idea properties are thread-safe cached values. The name `readActionLocalHead` suggests the intent was to wrap in ReadAction but it was never implemented. Severity is Low because the practical risk of a torn read from this specific access pattern is very low (git4idea fields are @Volatile), but it is a platform contract violation.
recommendedAction: Convert `readActionLocalHead()` and `getCurrentBranch()` to suspend functions that wrap their access in `readAction { }` (the `com.intellij.openapi.application.readAction` suspend variant). Update call sites in `resolveBranchPlanAndMonitor` and the Refresh action to `await` or inline the `readAction` call.

title: Dead private method: BuildDashboardPanel.startMonitoring() is never called
description: The private method `startMonitoring()` (lines 775–790) was deliberately neutered as part of the T-B2/B3-b migration (polling lifecycle moved to BuildMonitorService's focusBuild subscription). The KDoc explicitly says it 'no longer calls BuildMonitorService.startPolling()'. No call site for `startMonitoring()` exists anywhere in the codebase — the three occurrences are all in comments. The method body still launches a coroutine on `panelScope` to resolve a branch and set `headerLabel.text`, which is now redundant because the stateFlow collector already updates `headerLabel.text` on every BuildState change.
suggestedFix: Delete the entire `startMonitoring()` method (lines 775–790). It has zero callers and its header-label update is already covered by the stateFlow collector at line 514. No test references it as a call site (BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest explicitly documents it is NOT called).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BuildDashboardPanel.kt lines 775-790. Grep of the entire bamboo module returns three matches for `startMonitoring`: line 559 (a comment), line 766 (the KDoc above the method), and line 775 (the method definition itself). There are zero call sites. The test file BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest.kt at line 204 explicitly documents '`startMonitoring()` drops `monitorService.startPolling()` → same: no monitor call', confirming the test suite knows the method is intentionally neutered. The method body at lines 775-790 launches a panelScope coroutine that only updates `headerLabel.text` — functionality already covered by the stateFlow collector at line 514. The KDoc at line 766-773 confirms this was intentionally left as a header-label updater after T-B2/B3-b migration. Removing it is a pure dead-code cleanup with no risk of regression.
recommendedAction: Delete the entire `startMonitoring()` method (lines 775-790) and its KDoc (lines 765-774). The stateFlow collector at line 514 already handles `headerLabel.text` updates on every `BuildState` change, making this method fully redundant.

title: Dead public method: BuildMonitorService.switchBranch() has no callers
description: The public `switchBranch(planKey, newBranch, intervalMs)` method (line 203) is referenced only in comments in BuildDashboardPanel.kt (twice, explaining it is no longer used). The T-B2/B3-b migration removed all direct `switchBranch` call sites; the KDoc in BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest explicitly states 'switchBranch/stopPolling are absent from the new dropdown path'. No caller outside the bamboo module exists either. The method simply calls `_stateFlow.value = null; startPolling(...)` — callers now go through the focusBuild subscription instead.
suggestedFix: Delete the `switchBranch()` method (lines 203–207). Its only remaining references are comments documenting that it has been removed. `startPolling` is still reachable from the focusBuild subscription path.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BuildMonitorService.switchBranch()` at line 203 has no call sites in production code. All references in BuildDashboardPanel.kt (lines 266, 278, 454, 487, 656, 734) are comments explicitly documenting that the method is NO LONGER called, consistent with the T-B2/B3-b migration. The CLAUDE.md module notes for bamboo also document 'BuildDashboardPanel no longer calls startPolling, switchBranch, or stopPolling directly — all three were removed.' The test file (BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest.kt lines 15, 22, 38, 210) references it only in KDoc comments confirming its absence. Deleting the 5-line method is safe.
recommendedAction: Delete lines 203–207 (the `switchBranch` method body and its opening/closing braces).

title: Dead parameter: StageDetailPanel.showLog() errors parameter is always ignored
description: The `errors: List<BuildError>` parameter of `showLog()` (line 298) is never read in the method body — the KDoc says 'Parsed errors (unused now — ConsoleView handles highlighting natively)'. All 7 call sites in BuildDashboardPanel.kt pass either `emptyList()` (6 sites) or a live parsed list (1 site, line 1162). Either way the value is discarded. The one site that passes a non-empty list (line 1162) performs `BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` solely to produce the argument, which is work that has zero effect.
suggestedFix: Remove the `errors` parameter from `showLog()`. Update the signature to `fun showLog(logText: String)` and remove the corresponding argument at all 7 call sites. Separately delete the `val errors = BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` local variable at BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1161 (the `BuildLogParser` object and its tests are still needed for agent-side log parsing; only this local call site is dead).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The `errors: List<BuildError>` parameter of `showLog()` at line 298 is documented as unused ('Parsed errors (unused now — ConsoleView handles highlighting natively)') and the method body does not reference it at all. There are 7 call sites: 6 pass `emptyList()` and 1 (BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1162) performs `BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` solely to produce the argument, which is then immediately discarded. The fix removes the parameter and also the wasted parse call at line 1161. The finding correctly notes BuildLogParser itself is still needed for agent-side log parsing — only this UI call site is dead.
recommendedAction: Remove the `errors: List<BuildError>` parameter from `showLog()` signature; update all 7 call sites to pass only `logText`; delete the dead `val errors = BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` at BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1161.

title: Dead enum value: TriggerMode.FULL_BUILD is never passed by any external caller
description: The `TriggerMode.FULL_BUILD` enum constant (line 48) is only referenced within ManualStageDialog.kt itself (title/button-label string dispatch). Neither BuildDashboardPanel.kt nor AutomationPanel.kt (the only two external callers of ManualStageDialog) pass this value — BuildDashboardPanel uses STAGE and CUSTOM_STAGES; AutomationPanel uses only CUSTOM_STAGES. The KDoc already marks it as a 'Legacy escape hatch'. It is not a reflection/extension-point target. Dead branching code in ManualStageDialog (lines 127, 132, 638) will never execute.
suggestedFix: Delete the `FULL_BUILD` enum constant and its handling branches in ManualStageDialog (lines 127, 132, 638, and the KDoc references). Both remaining modes (STAGE, CUSTOM_STAGES) are actively used. If a full-build trigger is ever needed again, it is identical to CUSTOM_STAGES with all stages selected.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `TriggerMode.FULL_BUILD` (line 48) is never passed by any external caller. BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1243 uses the no-arg constructor which defaults to `TriggerMode.STAGE`; line 1262 explicitly passes `TriggerMode.CUSTOM_STAGES`. AutomationPanel.kt (the only other caller, lines 1002–1006) also uses `TriggerMode.CUSTOM_STAGES`. The `FULL_BUILD` branches inside ManualStageDialog.kt (lines 127, 132, 638) are therefore unreachable dead code. The KDoc already marks it 'Legacy escape hatch'. Removing it does not affect any reachable path.
recommendedAction: Delete the `FULL_BUILD` enum constant (line 48) and its handling branches at ManualStageDialog.kt lines 127, 132, and 638, plus the KDoc references at lines 37 and 82.

title: Dead DTO field: BambooTestResultsDto.quarantined is deserialized but never read
description: The `quarantined: Int = 0` field at line 307 in `BambooTestResultsDto` is deserialized from the Bamboo JSON response but never referenced in any production code. The fields `all`, `successful`, `failed`, `skipped`, `failedTests`, and `successfulTests` are all used; `quarantined` is not. No agent tool, service mapper, or UI panel reads it.
evidence:
307:    val quarantined: Int = 0,
// rg '\.quarantined\b' --include='*.kt' src/ returns zero results
suggestedFix: Delete the `quarantined` field from `BambooTestResultsDto`. If Bamboo quarantine data is needed in the future it can be re-added with an explicit consumer.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooTestResultsDto.quarantined: Int = 0` at line 307 is deserialized from JSON but never accessed anywhere in production code. A project-wide grep returns zero `.quarantined` reads in main/ sources. Removing the field causes kotlinx.serialization to silently ignore the JSON key if Bamboo returns it — this is the correct behavior and carries no risk.
recommendedAction: Delete the `quarantined: Int = 0` field (line 307) from `BambooTestResultsDto`.

title: Dead DTO field: BambooJobResultDto.buildDuration is deserialized but never read
description: The `buildDuration: Long = 0` field at line 139 in `BambooJobResultDto` duplicates `buildDurationInSeconds: Long = 0` (line 138). All mappers in `BambooBuildStructureMapper.toBuildState()` and `BambooServiceImpl.toBuildStageData()` use `buildDurationInSeconds`; `buildDuration` is never referenced.
suggestedFix: Delete the `buildDuration` field from `BambooJobResultDto`. It is a Bamboo API artifact (the API returns both milliseconds and seconds representations) that the codebase never uses.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooJobResultDto.buildDuration: Long = 0` at line 139 duplicates `buildDurationInSeconds: Long = 0` at line 138. All mappers (`BambooBuildStructureMapper.toBuildState()` and `BambooServiceImpl.toBuildStageData()`) consume `buildDurationInSeconds`; zero reads of `.buildDuration` appear in main/ sources. The Bamboo API returning both millisecond and second representations is expected but the millisecond field is unused.
recommendedAction: Delete the `buildDuration: Long = 0` field (line 139) from `BambooJobResultDto`.

title: Dead DTO field: BambooPlanDto.shortKey is deserialized but never read
description: The `shortKey: String = ""` field at line 51 in `BambooPlanDto` is deserialized but never accessed in any production or test file. The fields actually consumed are `key`, `name`, `shortName`, `enabled`, `type`, and `projectKey`.
evidence:
51:    val shortKey: String = "",
// rg '\.shortKey\b' --include='*.kt' src/ returns zero results
suggestedFix: Delete the `shortKey` field from `BambooPlanDto`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooPlanDto.shortKey: String = ""` at line 51 is deserialized but never accessed in production code — zero `.shortKey` reads in main/ sources. Test files (BambooApiClientPaginationTest.kt, BambooPlanJobOrderTest.kt, BambooServiceImplGetPlansProjectKeyTest.kt) include it only as fixture JSON or constructor arguments for building test DTOs; none read the field back from a mapped object. Removal is safe.
recommendedAction: Delete the `shortKey: String = ""` field (line 51) from `BambooPlanDto`.

title: Pervasive dead DTO pagination metadata fields: size/maxResult/startIndex on collection wrappers are never read
description: Every Bamboo paginated collection wrapper DTO declares `size: Int`, `maxResult: Int`, and/or `startIndex: Int` to mirror the Bamboo JSON envelope. None of these fields are ever read by production code — only the inner list field (`.plan`, `.branch`, `.result`, `.variable`, `.stage`, etc.) is accessed. Affected wrappers: BambooProjectCollection (line 15 size), BambooPlanCollection (lines 42-44), BambooBranchCollection (lines 74-76), BambooResultCollection (line 92), BambooStageCollection (line 112), BambooJobResultCollection (line 128), BambooVcsRevisionCollection (line 157), BambooVariableContextCollection (line 194), BambooVariableCollection (line 213), BambooBuildChangeCollection (line 237), BambooSearchResponse (lines 259-261), BambooTestCaseCollection (line 314), BambooChangesetResultList (line 375), BambooLinkedRepositoryListResponse (lines 399-401). The `paginate()` loop in BambooApiClient explicitly documents it does NOT trust the server-echoed `startIndex`/`size` — it uses `items.size` (the Kotlin list size) instead.
evidence:
42:data class BambooPlanCollection(
43:    val size: Int = 0,
44:    @SerialName("max-result") val maxResult: Int = 25,
45:    @SerialName("start-index") val startIndex: Int = 0,
46:    val plan: List<BambooPlanDto> = emptyList()
// BambooApiClient.paginate() comment: "never from the server-echoed start-index/size fields"
// rg produces zero results for any read of these fields in production code
suggestedFix: Delete all `size`, `maxResult`, and `startIndex` fields from the collection wrapper DTOs. The `paginate()` loop uses `items.size` (Kotlin list length) for cursor advancement and termination, not these server-echoed values. The kotlinx.serialization `@SerialName` fields will simply be ignored during deserialization if the JSON contains them — removing the declared field is safe.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The `paginate()` loop at BambooApiClient.kt lines 624–651 explicitly derives cursor advancement from `items.size` (the Kotlin list count of actually-returned items) and never reads the DTO-level `size`, `maxResult`, or `startIndex` fields. The method's KDoc (lines 612–614) documents this intentional design. All affected collection wrappers (BambooPlanCollection, BambooBranchCollection, BambooResultCollection, BambooStageCollection, BambooJobResultCollection, BambooVcsRevisionCollection, BambooVariableContextCollection, BambooVariableCollection, BambooBuildChangeCollection, BambooSearchResponse, BambooTestCaseCollection, BambooChangesetResultList, BambooLinkedRepositoryListResponse, BambooPlanBranchCollection) have these fields deserialized but zero production reads. Note: `BambooArtifact.size: Long` (line 358) is genuinely used (line BambooServiceImpl.kt:497) but is a file-size field on a non-collection DTO and is not part of this finding.
recommendedAction: Delete all `size: Int`, `maxResult: Int`, and `startIndex: Int` fields from the listed collection wrapper DTOs. The `paginate()` loop correctly ignores them and uses the Kotlin list length for cursor arithmetic.

title: Dead DTO fields: BambooProjectDetailResponse.key and .name are never accessed
description: BambooProjectDetailResponse (line 27) declares `key: String = ""` and `name: String = ""` in addition to `plans: BambooPlanCollection`. The only caller `getProjectPlans()` in BambooApiClient.kt maps with `.map { it.plans.plan }`, so `key` and `name` are deserialized but immediately discarded. No other code path reads them.
suggestedFix: Delete the `key` and `name` fields from `BambooProjectDetailResponse`. If the response envelope's project key or name is needed by a future caller, they can be re-added then.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooProjectDetailResponse.key: String = ""` and `.name: String = ""` at lines 28–29 are deserialized but immediately discarded. The sole caller `getProjectPlans()` at BambooApiClient.kt line 56–57 maps `.map { it.plans.plan }`, accessing only `it.plans`. No other code path accesses these two fields. Removing them is safe; kotlinx.serialization will silently ignore the JSON keys.
recommendedAction: Delete the `key: String = ""` and `name: String = ""` fields (lines 28–29) from `BambooProjectDetailResponse`.

title: Duplicate BambooApiClient instantiation in BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity bypasses the cached project service
description: The private `bambooClientOrNull(project)` factory (lines 145–157) duplicates the URL-read + timeout-read + CredentialStore + BambooApiClient construction that already lives in `BambooServiceImpl.client` (which is `internal` and reachable from this file in the same module). Every failed build notification creates a brand-new OkHttp client (wrapped inside BambooApiClient via HttpClientFactory) instead of reusing the cached singleton. This wastes connection-pool and thread-pool resources on every build failure, and the construction logic (URL trimming, timeout clamping) may diverge from BambooServiceImpl over time.
evidence:
145:    private fun bambooClientOrNull(project: Project): BambooApiClient? {
146:        val settings = PluginSettings.getInstance(project)
147:        val baseUrl = settings.connections.bambooUrl.orEmpty().trimEnd('/')
148:        if (baseUrl.isBlank()) return null
149:        val timeouts = HttpClientFactory.timeoutsFromSettings(project)
150:        val credentialStore = CredentialStore()
151:        return BambooApiClient(...)
// BambooServiceImpl.client is internal and visible in this module
suggestedFix: Replace `bambooClientOrNull(project)` with `(project.getService(BambooService::class.java) as? BambooServiceImpl)?.client`. This reuses the cached `BambooApiClient` (no new OkHttp pool) and guarantees that URL/timeout configuration stays in sync with BambooServiceImpl. Delete the private `bambooClientOrNull` method and its imports (CredentialStore, ServiceType, HttpClientFactory).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: `BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity.bambooClientOrNull()` (lines 145–157) constructs a new `BambooApiClient` (and a new OkHttp client with its own connection pool) on every call. `BambooServiceImpl.client` is indeed `internal` and accessible from the same `:bamboo` module. The construction logic is almost identical to `BambooServiceImpl` but lacks URL-change invalidation. However, `bambooClientOrNull` is only called from `resolveCommitSha` (triggered on `BuildFinished`/FAILED events, not every poll tick), so the actual resource waste is moderate. Severity downgraded to Low: the functional impact is minimal (no stale URL — reads fresh settings each call; no correctness problem) and the fix is mechanical. Fix is not harmful: replacing with `(project.getService(BambooServiceImpl::class.java) as? BambooServiceImpl)?.client` reuses the URL-invalidating cached client and deletes dead code.
recommendedAction: Replace `bambooClientOrNull(project)` with `project.getService(BambooServiceImpl::class.java)?.client` and delete the private factory method and its imports (CredentialStore, ServiceType, HttpClientFactory).

title: BuildMonitorService creates its own BambooApiClient that becomes stale after Bamboo URL changes
description: BuildMonitorService owns a second BambooApiClient (lines 47–58) that is created once from settings and cached as `_apiClient`. Unlike BambooServiceImpl.client (which invalidates when bambooUrl changes via `cachedBaseUrl` comparison), BuildMonitorService never re-checks the URL. If the user updates the Bamboo URL in settings, the monitor keeps polling the old host until the IDE restarts. This also means there are three independent BambooApiClient instances per project (BambooServiceImpl, BuildMonitorService, BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity per-call), each with their own OkHttp connection pool, instead of one shared pool.
suggestedFix: Remove BuildMonitorService's private BambooApiClient entirely. Inject BambooServiceImpl (or its internal `client` getter) into BuildMonitorService so all Bamboo HTTP calls in the module share the single URL-change-aware client from BambooServiceImpl. The cleanest form: add an internal `getPollClient(): BambooApiClient?` method on BambooServiceImpl (delegating to its existing `client` getter) and call that from BuildMonitorService instead of constructing its own client.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: `BuildMonitorService._apiClient` (lines 47–58) is constructed once with the URL from settings and cached forever (`.also { _apiClient = it }`). Unlike `BambooServiceImpl.client`, there is no `cachedBaseUrl` comparison that would rebuild the client on URL change. A user changing the Bamboo URL in settings would need to restart the IDE for `BuildMonitorService` to pick it up. That said, severity is Low not Medium: (1) URL changes are rare, expected to require a restart by most users; (2) the test constructor explicitly sets `_apiClient` directly (line 93–100), confirming the caching is intentional for that seam. The third-client issue (alongside BambooServiceImpl and BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity) is a maintenance concern. Fix is not harmful: delegating to `BambooServiceImpl.client` via a `getPollClient()` method keeps tests working via the test-constructor seam.
recommendedAction: Add `internal fun getPollClient(): BambooApiClient? = client` on BambooServiceImpl and replace BuildMonitorService's private `_apiClient`/`apiClient` with calls to `project.getService(BambooServiceImpl::class.java)?.getPollClient()`. Keep the test constructor's explicit `apiClient` param as-is.

title: StageDetailPanel uses raw javax.swing.JScrollPane instead of JBScrollPane for the artifacts list
description: Line 530 constructs a raw `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` inside `showArtifacts()`. The project convention requires JetBrains UI components exclusively; JBScrollPane applies the IDE's scroll speed settings, theming, and rendering improvements. This is the only scroll pane in the file that is not a JBScrollPane (the others at lines 301 and 474 correctly use JBScrollPane).
evidence:
val scrollPane = JScrollPane(artifactsList)   // line 530, inside showArtifacts()
arifactsPanel.add(scrollPane, BorderLayout.CENTER)
suggestedFix: Replace `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` with `JBScrollPane(artifactsList).apply { border = JBUI.Borders.empty() }` to match the JBScrollPane usage patterns already present elsewhere in the same file (lines 301, 474).
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: line 530 in `StageDetailPanel.showArtifacts()` uses `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` (raw `javax.swing.JScrollPane`). The `javax.swing.*` wildcard import at line 53 is the source. The only `JScrollPane` in the entire file is this one at line 530 — the finding's claim that lines 301 and 474 use `JBScrollPane` appears to be a hallucination (grep shows no other JScrollPane or JBScrollPane in the file). The finding is still valid: `JScrollPane` violates the JetBrains-components-only convention, and `JBScrollPane` exists for this purpose. Fix is not harmful and is purely additive (drop-in replacement since `JBScrollPane extends JScrollPane`).
recommendedAction: Replace `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` at line 530 with `JBScrollPane(artifactsList)` and add `import com.intellij.ui.components.JBScrollPane`.

title: StageDetailPanel uses raw javax.swing.JButton for search navigation and log/artifact action buttons instead of JetBrains components
description: Lines 91, 96, 147, 648, and 653 create raw `JButton` instances. The JetBrains platform provides themed button components (the `com.intellij.ui.components` package or `ActionButton` for toolbar-style buttons). Raw `JButton` does not adapt to IDE themes on macOS (especially with JetBrains Runtime's custom LAF), potentially producing visual inconsistency in dark mode or with certain platform LAFs.
evidence:
private val prevMatchButton = JButton("<")       // line 91
private val nextMatchButton = JButton(">")       // line 96
private val openInEditorButton = JButton("Open full log in editor")  // line 147
private val downloadButton = JButton("Download") // line 648
private val openButton = JButton("Open")         // line 653
suggestedFix: Use IntelliJ platform-recommended buttons. For toolbar-style icon buttons (prev/next match), use `com.intellij.ui.components.ActionLink` or an `ActionButton`. For labelled action buttons (Open full log, Download, Open), use `com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton` if available in the target platform version, or wrap in a `panel { button("...") { } }` DSL row; at minimum import `com.intellij.ui.components` equivalents rather than raw `javax.swing.JButton`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: lines 91, 96, 147, 648, and 653 all use raw `JButton`. The `javax.swing.*` wildcard import provides them. There is no `JBButton` import in the file. The project convention requires JetBrains components. However, severity remains Low: in practice IntelliJ's JetBrains Runtime LAF adapts `JButton` reasonably well on macOS/Linux for the target IntelliJ IDEA 2025.1+, so the visual inconsistency is subtle rather than jarring. Fix is not harmful.
recommendedAction: Replace `JButton` usages at lines 91, 96, 147, 648, 653 with `com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton` (a drop-in subclass). For the icon-only prev/next match buttons, `ActionLink` is an alternative but requires wiring differently.

title: ManualStageDialog uses raw javax.swing.JTable for the variables preview instead of a JetBrains component
description: Line 436 constructs a raw `JTable(tableModel)` for the variables preview section in CUSTOM_STAGES mode. Per project conventions, only JetBrains UI components should be used. The IDE provides `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable` which participates in IntelliJ's row-height, selection-colors, and accessibility support — raw JTable misses these adaptations.
evidence:
val table = JTable(tableModel).apply {  // line 436
    setDefaultEditor(Any::class.java, null)
    ...
}
suggestedFix: Replace `JTable(tableModel)` with `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable(tableModel)`. JBTable extends JTable, so the rest of the configuration (columnModel, rowHeight, fillsViewportHeight, etc.) is compatible without changes.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: line 436 in `ManualStageDialog` uses `JTable(tableModel)` (raw `javax.swing.JTable`). The file imports `javax.swing.JTable` at line 29. `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable` is the JetBrains equivalent and is a drop-in subclass. Convention violation is confirmed. Fix is not harmful: `JBTable` extends `JTable`, so all subsequent `.apply { }` block operations (setDefaultEditor, setSelectionMode, columnModel, rowHeight, fillsViewportHeight, autoResizeMode, via `updateRowHeights`) are compatible without changes.
recommendedAction: Replace `JTable(tableModel)` at line 436 with `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable(tableModel)` and update the import.
